title: Landing positioning is tech-only — a distinguished chef is told this product isn't for him before he types a word
expected: The positioning/FAQ doesn't read as tech-only; an arts/culinary petitioner sees his field is supported (acceptance criterion 5).
got: The landing masthead ribbon names the audience as 'For founders · engineers · researchers · designers' (no artists/chefs), and the hero subhead says the petition is drafted from 'your CV, GitHub, press and publications'. A chef has no GitHub or publications; the front door reads as not-for-me. This is Diego's first-stated question ('built for someone like me, or just for tech people?') answered in the wrong direction.
suggested_acceptance: Landing audience/evidence copy includes the arts (e.g. 'founders, researchers, artists & performers' and 'CV, press, reviews, awards') so an O-1B arts petitioner sees himself; remove or balance the GitHub-only framing.
l2_priority: Confirm live the landing copy still reads tech-only and judge whether an arts visitor bounces before reaching the (correct) O-1B selector.

title: Marketing describes only 'the eight O-1 criteria' — never the O-1B arts six — so an arts petitioner can't tell he won't be screened on the O-1A eight
expected: Diego can tell, before spending a token, that a chef is screened as O-1B arts against the six (lead role / recognition / reviews & press / commercial-critical success / org-expert recognition / high salary), not the O-1A eight (acceptance criteria 2, 5).
got: Every marketing surface frames the product as 'the eight O-1 criteria'; even the /qualify page <title>/meta says 'An informational O-1A self-screening … the eight … criteria'. O-1B arts is a six-criterion program, but the public copy never says so. The visible on-page /qualify body copy does name O-1A/O-1B/EB-1A and the FAQ mentions O-1A/O-1B — so it's not absent everywhere — but the dominant framing conflates O-1 with the O-1A eight, leaving Diego's sharpest fear (mis-classification as O-1A) unanswered from the front door.
suggested_acceptance: Marketing/meta copy distinguishes the programs (O-1A eight, O-1B arts six, EB-1A ten) at least once where an arts petitioner will see it; the /qualify <title>/description stops calling the multi-program funnel an 'O-1A … eight criteria' screen.
l2_priority: Verify live the public copy never states the arts six; judge whether an arts prospect perceives mis-classification risk.

title: First-impression verdict (hero + best-path) is the keyword mock, whose O-1B regexes under-fire on chef wording — risking a hollow under-read before the real model is reached
expected: The screener returns a substantive, evidence-named O-1B verdict for a chef, not a hollow under-read born of not understanding culinary evidence (acceptance criteria 1, 3).
got: Both the anonymous Instant Verdict (/api/qualify/preview → mockQualification) and the BestPathFinder (best-path.ts scores EVERY program with mockQualification, even when a model is configured) score by regex keyword presence only. For a chef the O-1B regexes are uneven: 'Record of major commercial or critical success' (box office|chart|sales|streams|…) and 'Recognition from organizations & experts' (guild|academy|society|expert|…) do not fire on 'James Beard'/'Michelin'/'guest-chef'; 'Lead role in distinguished productions' needs literal 'lead role'/'leading role', so 'executive chef leading the kitchen' misses. The first verdict Diego sees can under-count a genuinely strong arts record. The real model on authenticated /api/qualify fixes this, but he only reaches it after the soft sign-in.
suggested_acceptance: The hero/best-path keyword fallback recognises arts/culinary recognition tokens (Michelin, James Beard, guest chef, residency, tasting menu) for the O-1B criteria, OR the best-path uses the model when one is configured so an arts profile isn't under-scored at first contact.
l2_priority: Run the real chef profile through both the keyless preview and the authenticated model; confirm the model path maps Beard/Michelin/NYT to the arts six and the mock's first read isn't damagingly thin.

title: No profession/culinary context reaches the qualify prompt — arts-domain mapping is left entirely to the model's general knowledge
expected: His real evidence is named and correctly mapped: Beard/Michelin → recognition/awards; NYT/Bon Appétit → reviews & press; leading a distinguished kitchen / guest-chef events → lead role (acceptance criterion 3).
got: buildQualifyPrompt is profession-agnostic: it sends the full profile + the six O-1B criterion names, but no culinary scaffolding telling the model 'a Michelin mention is recognition, a NYT dining review is reviews & press, a guest-chef residency is a lead role in a distinguished production'. Grounding is otherwise good (full profile reaches the prompt), so this is a richer-context opportunity, not a broken path; correctness rests on the model knowing the culinary→criterion crosswalk unaided.
suggested_acceptance: Either pass a profession/domain hint into the qualify prompt, or add a culinary entry to the profession content map and thread it, so culinary evidence is explicitly crosswalked to the arts six.
l2_priority: Assert the live O-1B draft/verdict actually names Diego's Michelin/Beard/NYT evidence under the right criterion without prompting hints.

title: No 'chef'/culinary SEO page — /visa/o-1b/chef is a hard 404; the generated professions are tech/arts-generic
expected: A culinary petitioner Googling 'O-1B visa for chefs' finds a tuned page that says his field is handled (supports JTBD 'does this handle chefs at all').
got: /visa/[classification]/[profession] resolves only the 5 generated professions (software-engineer, researcher, founder, designer, artist); 'chef' is not among them, so resolve() returns null and the page calls notFound() — a hard 404. The closest, /visa/o-1b/artist, exists but carries generic arts examples with no culinary specifics. 'chef'/'culinary'/'Michelin'/'James Beard' appear nowhere in src.
suggested_acceptance: Add a 'chef'/'culinary-artist' profession to the content map (O-1B examples: Michelin/Beard → recognition; NYT/Bon Appétit → reviews & press; flagship kitchen / guest-chef events → lead role; comp → high salary), so the SEO matrix and any profession-tuned copy cover culinary.
l2_priority: Confirm /visa/o-1b/chef behaviour live (404) and whether the artist page is an acceptable nearest match; low priority vs the core flow.

title: O-1B arts pack is correct and reachable — arts six, exact criterion names, 3-of-6 threshold, no silent O-1A fallback when O-1B is selected
expected: An arts Character is screened against the O-1B six, not the O-1A eight, with no fallback-to-O-1A (protocol's pack-correctness check).
got: VISA_PACKS['O-1B'] holds the six arts criteria with names matching Diego's natural evidence buckets (Lead role in distinguished productions / National or international recognition / Reviews & press / Record of major commercial or critical success / Recognition from organizations & experts / High salary or remuneration), threshold 3, matching the verified validation record (3 of 6). O-1B is a live program, so the selector offers it on every entry surface; selecting it threads the six end-to-end. packFor()'s O-1A fallback fires only on a missing/unknown classification, which the explicit <select> never sends — no silent mis-classification.
suggested_acceptance: Keep as-is; protect the pack model and the live-program gating.

title: Validation page names O-1B as live+verified with real cited evidence (8 CFR 214.2(o)(3)(iv), 3-of-6, USCIS Policy Manual Vol.2 Part M — Diego's own reference)
expected: The validation/correctness page shows evidence, not adjectives, and credibly answers 'do you handle my program?'.
got: PROGRAM_VALIDATIONS['O-1B'] is status 'verified', legal basis 8 CFR 214.2(o)(3)(iv), threshold '3 of 6 criteria (or a qualifying major award/nomination)', with a last-reviewed date, freshness meter, and primary-law + agency-guidance source links including USCIS Policy Manual Vol. 2 Part M — the exact reference in Diego's character file. Rendered on /validation per live program.
suggested_acceptance: Keep as-is; protect the validation framework and the O-1B record.
